one.zh: 上诉待决期间的中止与回避申请:法院准予中止「责令诉讼监护人说明何以不应被免职」的命令(因上诉真诚、不久即审、对方无实质损害),但驳回基于偏见/表见偏见的回避申请。
one.en: A stay-pending-appeal and disqualification application: the Court granted a stay of the order calling on the litigation guardian to show cause why she should not be removed (the appeal being bona fide, soon to be heard, with no real prejudice to the respondent), but dismissed the application that the judge disqualify himself for bias or apprehended bias.

issue.zh: 其一,在上诉待决期间,是否应中止「责令诉讼监护人说明理由」的命令?其二,申请人是否确立了据以要求法官回避的偏见或表见偏见基础?
issue.en: First, whether the order calling on the litigation guardian to show cause should be stayed pending appeal; second, whether the applicant established any basis of bias or apprehended bias requiring the judge to disqualify himself.

subseq.zh: 上诉预计于六月开庭期由全席法庭审理;命令 2 中止至上诉裁定为止,其余 2026 年 3 月 16 日程序内申请被驳回。法官并指出 2026 年 3 月 5 日判决封面所载聆讯日期有误,应予更正。
subseq.en: The appeal is expected to be heard by a Full Court in the June sittings; order 2 is stayed until the appeal is determined, and the balance of the Application in a Proceeding filed 16 March 2026 is dismissed. The judge also noted an error on the cover sheet of the 5 March 2026 reasons as to the hearing date, to be corrected.

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – Practice and procedure – Application for stay pending appeal – Where the applicant seeks to stay order calling upon his litigation guardian to show cause why she should not be removed – Order stayed pending disposition of appeal. FAMILY LAW – Practice and procedure – Application for disqualification – Where the applicant makes an application for disqualification on the basis of bias or apprehension of bias – Where the applicant does not establish any basis for disqualification – Wife’s application for disqualification dismissed.

hint.en: The whole case as a story

html.zh:
本案是一场诉讼内的程序之争。此前在 2026 年 3 月 5 日,Jarrett 法官驳回了由申请人之诉讼监护人 Ms B Bongiovani 依《家庭法法》第 102NA(1)(c)(iv) 条提出的申请,并另行命令该诉讼监护人于 2026 年 4 月 13 日上午 9:30 说明理由:何以不应将其免去诉讼监护人之职,或退而求其次,何以不应将程序中止至为申请人聘任并指示律师为止。

诉讼监护人已于 2026 年 3 月 16 日提交上诉通知,寻求就上述两项命令上诉的许可;同日她又提出程序内申请,请求:(a) 在上诉结果出来前,中止那道「说明理由」的命令;(b) 法官就「本程序的后续处理」回避。申请人(经其诉讼监护人)不反对法院先决中止申请、再处理回避问题。

Jarrett 法官分两部分处理。中止:他认定上诉真诚、若不中止上诉恐成空文(监护人可能已被免职),对方除时间流逝外未指出任何损害,且上诉很可能于六月开庭期由全席法庭审理、延宕甚短;尽管他评估上诉前景并不强(但非毫无),仍认为中止属适当。回避:他认为申请人未能指出任何会使其不依法律与事实裁判的事项,亦未在所诉事项与「可能不持公正之心」之间建立逻辑关联,故驳回回避申请。
html.en:
This is a procedural dispute within ongoing litigation. Earlier, on 5 March 2026, Jarrett J dismissed an application brought by the applicant's litigation guardian, Ms B Bongiovani, under s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) of the Family Law Act, and separately ordered the litigation guardian to show cause at 9.30 am on 13 April 2026 why she should not be removed as litigation guardian, or alternatively why the proceedings should not be stayed until legal representation was engaged and instructed for the applicant.

The litigation guardian filed a Notice of Appeal on 16 March 2026 seeking leave to appeal both orders; the same day she filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking: (a) a stay of the show-cause order pending the appeal; and (b) that the judge disqualify himself from further dealing with the proceedings. The applicant, by his litigation guardian, had no difficulty with the stay being decided first.

Jarrett J dealt with the matter in two parts. Stay: he found the appeal bona fide, that the appeal might be rendered nugatory if no stay were granted (the guardian could be removed), that the respondent pointed to no prejudice beyond a lapse of time, and that the appeal was likely to be heard by a Full Court in the June sittings, so the delay was short; though he assessed the appeal's prospects as not strong (but not non-existent), a stay was appropriate. Disqualification: he found the applicant identified nothing that might lead him to decide other than on the legal and factual merits, and drew no logical connection between the complaints and any feared departure from impartiality, so the application was dismissed.

html.zh:
性质:诉讼内的程序申请——上诉待决期间的中止申请,加上回避申请,由 Jarrett 法官于 2026 年 4 月 14 日在布里斯班裁定。

时间线:2026-03-05 法官驳回第 102NA 申请并发出「说明理由」命令 → 2026-03-16 诉讼监护人提交上诉通知,并提出中止+回避的程序内申请 → 2026-04-14 本裁定。上诉预计于六月开庭期由全席法庭审理。
html.en:
Nature: interlocutory procedural applications within the proceedings — a stay pending appeal, together with a disqualification application — decided by Jarrett J at Brisbane on 14 April 2026.

Timeline: 5 Mar 2026, the judge dismissed the s 102NA application and made the show-cause order → 16 Mar 2026, the litigation guardian filed a Notice of Appeal and an Application in a Proceeding seeking a stay and disqualification → 14 Apr 2026, this decision. The appeal is expected to be heard by a Full Court in the June sittings.

html.zh:
§ s102NA ›
§ s57 ›
框架
本案涉两条制定法:《家庭法法》第 102NA(1)(c)(iv) 条(此前被驳回之申请所依据,关乎个人盘问的安排)与《联邦巡回与家事法院法》第 57 条(规定除合资格之大律师/事务律师等外,当事人无权由他人代理)。中止与回避两项裁断本身则依判例确立的检验展开——见「判例」。
html.en:
§ s102NA ›
§ s57 ›
Framework
Two statutory provisions feature: s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) of the Family Law Act (the basis of the earlier, dismissed application, concerning arrangements for personal cross-examination) and s 57 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act (a party is not entitled to be represented by another person except by a qualified barrister/solicitor and like exceptions). The stay and disqualification questions themselves turn on case-law tests — see Cases.

html.zh: 法院援引三件判例。Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy(高等法院,2000)确立表见偏见的检验——「持平的旁观者是否可能合理地担忧法官未能以公正之心裁判」,并采两步分析。Henley & Bestari(2024)进一步说明两步分析中「第一步」(指出可能使裁判者偏离法律事实之事项)的运用。F, M v Attorney-General's Department(南澳地区法院,2013)阐述诉讼监护人的职责与地位——其须依法律意见行事,一般不得亲自出庭。
html.en: Three authorities are cited. Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy (High Court, 2000) states the apprehended-bias test — whether a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend that the judge might not bring an impartial mind — applied as a two-step analysis. Henley & Bestari (2024) elaborates the first step (identifying what might lead a decision-maker to decide otherwise than on the merits). F, M v Attorney-General's Department (SA District Court, 2013) describes the office, duties and standing of a litigation guardian — who must act under legal advice and is generally not entitled to appear personally.

html.zh:
1. 中止的检验 —— 对方虽享有命令之利益、命令亦推定正确,但法院仍可在有适当基础时中止上诉所涉命令;申请人无须证明「特殊」或「例外」情形。法院综合衡量:上诉是否真诚、便利之平衡与双方权利、不中止是否致上诉成空、对上诉强弱的初步评估,以及上诉何时能审等。

2. 适用于本案的中止 —— 上诉真诚;若不中止,监护人可能先被免职而使上诉成空(纵可于上诉成功后重任,意义不大);对方除时间流逝外无损害;上诉很可能于六月由全席法庭审理,延宕甚短。法官评估上诉前景不强但非毫无。权衡之下,中止 2026 年 3 月 5 日命令 2 属适当。

3. 回避的检验 —— 依 Ebner:若持平的旁观者可能合理担忧法官不持公正之心,即应回避;采两步分析,第一步须指出可能使裁判者偏离法律事实之事项。

4. 适用于本案的回避 —— 监护人所诉(如被告知不得在终庭代理申请人、感到被「贬低」、对程序的误解等)缺乏特定细节,且其中多项陈述对程序存在基本误解(申请实在公开法庭审理、双方均获陈词机会、裁定在公开法庭宣布)。法院认为申请人既未指出任何会使其偏离法律事实裁判之事项,纵使指出,也未在该等事项与「可能不持公正之心」间建立逻辑关联。回避申请被驳回。
html.en:
1. The stay test — although the respondent has the benefit of the order and the order is presumed correct, the court may stay the order under appeal if a proper basis is shown; no 'special' or 'exceptional' circumstances need be demonstrated. The court weighs the bona fides of the appeal, the balance of convenience and competing rights, the risk the appeal would be rendered nugatory, a preliminary assessment of the appeal's strength, and when it might be heard.

2. The stay applied — the appeal is bona fide; without a stay the guardian might be removed and the appeal rendered nugatory (re-appointment after success offering little advantage); the respondent showed no prejudice beyond a lapse of time; and the appeal is likely to be heard by a Full Court in June, so the delay is short. The judge assessed the prospects as not strong but not non-existent. On balance, a stay of order 2 of 5 March 2026 was appropriate.

3. The disqualification test — per Ebner, a judge is disqualified if a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend the judge might not bring an impartial mind; applied through a two-step analysis, the first step being to identify what might lead the decision-maker to decide otherwise than on the merits.

4. Disqualification applied — the guardian's complaints (being told she could not appear for the applicant at the final hearing, feeling 'belittled', misunderstandings of procedure) lacked particularity, and several reflected a basic misunderstanding (the application was in fact heard in open court, both parties had the chance to make submissions, and the decision was delivered in open court). The applicant identified no matter that might lead the judge to decide other than on the merits and, even if any did, drew no logical connection to a feared departure from impartiality. The application was dismissed.

html.zh:
裁判要旨

上诉待决期间的中止,无须证明特殊或例外情形;只要上诉真诚、不中止恐使上诉成空、对方无实质损害、且上诉不久即可审理,即便上诉前景不强,法院亦可裁量准予中止。

回避申请须经 Ebner 两步检验:既要指出可能使裁判者偏离法律事实之事项,又要在该事项与「可能不持公正之心」之间建立逻辑关联;对裁决结果不满或对法官态度有怨,本身不足以构成表见偏见。
html.en:
Ratio

A stay pending appeal does not require special or exceptional circumstances; where the appeal is bona fide, would be rendered nugatory without a stay, the respondent suffers no real prejudice, and the appeal will soon be heard, the court may grant a stay in its discretion even though the appeal's prospects are not strong.

A disqualification application must satisfy the two-step Ebner test: identifying what might lead the decision-maker to decide otherwise than on the merits, and articulating a logical connection between that and the feared departure from impartiality. Dissatisfaction with a decision, or grievance about a judge's manner, is not itself enough to establish apprehended bias.

html.zh:
Litigation guardian · 诉讼监护人
代当事人进行诉讼的法院官员,须依法律意见行事,一般不得亲自出庭(Litigation guardian: a court officer who conducts litigation for a party, must act under legal advice and generally cannot appear personally).
Stay pending appeal · 上诉待决中止
在上诉裁定前暂停所涉命令的效力(Stay pending appeal: suspension of an order's operation until the appeal is decided).
Show cause · 说明理由
法院责令一方说明何以不应作出某项命令的程序(Show cause: a procedure requiring a party to explain why an order should not be made).
Apprehended bias · 表见偏见
持平旁观者可能合理担忧法官未能持公正之心的情形(Apprehended bias: where a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend a judge might not bring an impartial mind).
Nugatory · 成空
因未予中止等原因,使上诉即便成功亦失去实际意义(Nugatory: where, absent a stay, an appeal would be rendered pointless even if successful).
Bona fide · 真诚
出于善意、非为拖延的(此处指上诉真诚)(Bona fide: in good faith, not for delay — here, that the appeal is genuine).
html.en:
Litigation guardian
A court officer who conducts litigation for a party; must act under legal advice and generally cannot appear personally.
Stay pending appeal
Suspension of an order's operation until the appeal is decided.
Show cause
A procedure requiring a party to explain why a particular order should not be made.
Apprehended bias
Where a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend a judge might not bring an impartial mind.
Nugatory
Rendered pointless — here, where without a stay a successful appeal would be of no practical effect.
Bona fide
In good faith, not for delay — here, that the appeal is genuine.

html.zh:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J:

On 5 March 2026 I made an order dismissing an application by the litigation guardian for the applicant for an order under s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth). Further, I ordered that the applicant’s litigation guardian, Ms B Bongiovani, show cause at 9.30 am on 13 April 2026 why:

(a) she should not be removed as a litigation guardian for the applicant; or alternatively

(b) why the proceedings should not be stayed until such time as legal representation has been engaged and instructed for the applicant.

Although the notice of appeal is not in evidence before me, Ms B Bongiovani has sought leave to appeal both of those orders by a Notice of Appeal filed on 16 March 2026.

By her Application in a Proceeding filed on 16 March 2026, she now seeks:

(a) pending the outcome of her appeal, a stay of the order requiring her to show cause why she should not be removed as the applicant’s litigation guardian; and

(b) an order that I disqualify myself from “further dealing with these Proceedings”.

The applicant, by his litigation guardian, seemed to have no difficulty with me first deciding the stay application before then going on to determine if I should disqualify myself.

The stay application

Whilst the respondent is entitled to the benefit of the order I have made (in the broadest sense given the nature of the order under appeal) and that order is presumed to be correct, if Ms B Bongiovani establishes a proper basis for the stay, the court may stay the operation of the order under appeal. It is not necessary for the applicant to demonstrate any “special” or “exceptional” circumstances to secure the stay. Taking into account the bona fides of the appeal, the balance of convenience and the competing rights of the parties, the risk that an appeal may be rendered nugatory if a stay is not granted, some preliminary assessment of the strength of the appeal and the practical arrangements for the hearing of the appeal (such as when it might be heard) , the court may grant the stay if it is satisfied that it is appropriate to do so.

I am satisfied that the appeal is bona fide. If the stay is not granted, the appeal might be nugatory because the applicant’s litigation guardian may have been removed. She could, of course, be re-appointed in the event of a successful appeal, but there seems to be little advantage to such a course. The respondent points to no prejudice arising from a delay in dealing with the show cause procedure other than a lapse of time. On that point, having made enquiries, I am informed that the appeal is likely to be listed for hearing before a Full Court in the June sittings. Accordingly, the delay until the hearing of the appeal is relatively short.

As to the merits of the appeal, the basis upon which I determined that the applicant’s litigation guardian should show cause why she should not be removed are set out in my reasons delivered on 5 March 2026. In his proposed Notice of Appeal, the applicant particularises two findings said to be erroneous, namely:

(a) “The evidence provided by the Applicant was sufficient and as such she was wrongly deemed incompetent”.

(b) “The evidence regarding the intentions of Judicial Registrar Brooks allowing the Litigation Guardian to act at the trial in the event that a solicitor was not engaged was quite clear.”

As to the first matter, my reasons show that I did not consider that the application for an order pursuant to s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) was sufficient to secure that order. I described the application as “hopeless” for that reason. As to the litigation guardian’s competence, I said at [14]:

The evidence used to support it was entirely inadequate but, yet, proper legal advice and proper legal representation might have seen a different outcome, although I suspect not, but one never knows. The applicant in the proceedings, the person for whom Ms [B Bongiovani] acts as litigation guardian, has been denied the opportunity of having a successful outcome on that application because of her incompetence.

The only evidence relied upon by the applicant was an affidavit by the litigation guardian in which she swore “I can confirm that the Applicant has been the subject to domestic abuse by the Respondent Wife”. An Application for an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order was annexed to the affidavit, but the application was not signed and its contents neither sworn nor sworn to.

The office of litigation guardian (or next friend, tutor or guardian ad litem depending upon the era, court and role) is ancient. A litigation guardian is an officer of the court and the duties of that office are described in Daniell’s Chancery Practice (Sweet & Maxwell, 8th ed, 1985) at pages 100 and 101. Moreover, the duties and responsibilities of a litigation guardian (or someone in a concomitant role) were described in F, M v Attorney-General's Department [2013] SADC 123, particularly at [24].

The second matter, said to be a finding, was that a judicial registrar had apparently authorised the litigation guardian to appear in court to represent the applicant. However, the order appointing the litigation guardian makes no such provision. Whatever statements may have been made by the judicial registrar or those that assist her, the position at law, generally speaking, is that a litigation guardian must act under legal advice and is not entitled to appear personally before the court (see F, M v Attorney-General's Department at [24] and the authorities there cited).

Moreover, by s 57 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) a party to a proceeding before this court is not entitled to be represented by another person unless:

(a) under the Judiciary Act 1903, the other person is entitled to practise as a barrister or solicitor, or both, in a federal court; or

(b) under the regulations, the other person is taken to be an authorised representative; or

(c) another law of the Commonwealth authorises the other person to represent the party.

The litigation guardian is not a legal practitioner. There are no regulations that confer a right of audience upon her and the general law is that a litigation guardian must appear by a solicitor.

I cannot conclude that the applicant’s prospects of securing leave to appeal and then a successful appeal are strong. They are not non-existent, but it seems to me that the proposed appeal is not strong.

Nonetheless, taking into account the bona fides of the appeal, the short period until the appeal is likely to be heard, the nature of the order under appeal and the absence of any likely prejudice to the respondent, I consider that it is appropriate to grant a stay of order 2 made on 5 March 2026 until the determination of the applicant’s appeal against that order.

Disqualification

In Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy [2000] HCA 63; (2000) 205 CLR 337 the plurality of the High Court of Australia said (at [6]):

... a judge is disqualified if a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend that the judge might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the question the judge is required to decide. That principle gives effect to the requirement that justice should both be done and be seen to be done, a requirement which reflects the fundamental importance of the principle that the tribunal be independent and impartial. It is convenient to refer to it as the apprehension of bias principle.

(Footnotes omitted)

The question is whether a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend that the decision-maker might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the issue they are to decide. The answer to the question involves a two-step analysis.

The first step is to identify what it is said might lead a decision maker to decide a case other than on its legal and factual merits (Ebner at [8], Henley & Bestari (2024) FLC 94–169 at [25]).

As to this, in an affidavit in support of the application, the litigation guardian suggests that at a directions hearing on 20 January 2026, I informed the litigation guardian that she could not appear for the applicant at the final hearing. Although there is no transcript of the hearing before me, I accept that I probably said such a thing. The litigation guardian says that I raised my voice with her and “continually belittled me”. She gives no particulars of what it is that I am said to have said to her, other than that she could not appear for the applicant at the final hearing.

The applicant next complains that I did not consider all of the material she put before me on the application for the s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) application. Whether or not I did is a matter for the appeal against my dismissal of that order. I do not consider that it is a matter that goes to the question now under consideration.

The litigation guardian swears that on 3 February 2026, at the hearing of the application of the s 102NA order, the respondent’s solicitor suggested that Ms B Bongiovani should not be the litigation guardian for the applicant. She suggests that I agreed. There is no transcript of the exchange. She swears, “This was awful. These 2 were talking that way knowing I would find it offensive and knowing the Applicant would find it very upsetting.” There are no particulars.

The litigation guardian further swears that I said that I would reserve my decision on the application. But, she says, “Such Application clearly stated that it will be heard in an open Court but this never occurred as HH made His decision in chambers. In doing so I was never given a chance to expand on my Affidavit if need be”. None of this, however, is correct. The application was heard in open court and both parties were given the opportunity to make submissions in support of their respective positions. I reserved my decision. Something which is entirely orthodox. I delivered my decision in open court in the presence of the parties on 5 March 2026. These statements by the litigation guardian demonstrate a basic misunderstanding of procedure.

The litigation guardian makes other assertions in her affidavit about various matters, none of which have any particularity.

I do not consider that the applicant has identified any matter that is said might lead me to decide this case other than on its legal and factual merits.

Even if I am wrong about that and any of the matters referred to above, taken either separately or as a whole, satisfy the first step of the test, the applicant does not demonstrate a logical connection between those matters and the feared departure from deciding the case on its merits. The applicant’s litigation guardian feels aggrieved by my decision on the s 102NA application and complains about my manner but draws no connection between them and the real possibility that I might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the question I am likely to be required to decide.

The application that I disqualify myself from further involvement in these proceedings will be dismissed.

In the course of preparation of these reasons, it has become apparent that there is an error on the cover sheet of the reasons for judgment delivered on 5 March 2026. That sheet records the date of hearing as 5 March 2026. The hearing in fact occurred on 3 February 2026 and the cover sheet should be amended accordingly.
html.en:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J:

On 5 March 2026 I made an order dismissing an application by the litigation guardian for the applicant for an order under s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth). Further, I ordered that the applicant’s litigation guardian, Ms B Bongiovani, show cause at 9.30 am on 13 April 2026 why:

(a) she should not be removed as a litigation guardian for the applicant; or alternatively

(b) why the proceedings should not be stayed until such time as legal representation has been engaged and instructed for the applicant.

Although the notice of appeal is not in evidence before me, Ms B Bongiovani has sought leave to appeal both of those orders by a Notice of Appeal filed on 16 March 2026.

By her Application in a Proceeding filed on 16 March 2026, she now seeks:

(a) pending the outcome of her appeal, a stay of the order requiring her to show cause why she should not be removed as the applicant’s litigation guardian; and

(b) an order that I disqualify myself from “further dealing with these Proceedings”.

The applicant, by his litigation guardian, seemed to have no difficulty with me first deciding the stay application before then going on to determine if I should disqualify myself.

The stay application

Whilst the respondent is entitled to the benefit of the order I have made (in the broadest sense given the nature of the order under appeal) and that order is presumed to be correct, if Ms B Bongiovani establishes a proper basis for the stay, the court may stay the operation of the order under appeal. It is not necessary for the applicant to demonstrate any “special” or “exceptional” circumstances to secure the stay. Taking into account the bona fides of the appeal, the balance of convenience and the competing rights of the parties, the risk that an appeal may be rendered nugatory if a stay is not granted, some preliminary assessment of the strength of the appeal and the practical arrangements for the hearing of the appeal (such as when it might be heard) , the court may grant the stay if it is satisfied that it is appropriate to do so.

I am satisfied that the appeal is bona fide. If the stay is not granted, the appeal might be nugatory because the applicant’s litigation guardian may have been removed. She could, of course, be re-appointed in the event of a successful appeal, but there seems to be little advantage to such a course. The respondent points to no prejudice arising from a delay in dealing with the show cause procedure other than a lapse of time. On that point, having made enquiries, I am informed that the appeal is likely to be listed for hearing before a Full Court in the June sittings. Accordingly, the delay until the hearing of the appeal is relatively short.

As to the merits of the appeal, the basis upon which I determined that the applicant’s litigation guardian should show cause why she should not be removed are set out in my reasons delivered on 5 March 2026. In his proposed Notice of Appeal, the applicant particularises two findings said to be erroneous, namely:

(a) “The evidence provided by the Applicant was sufficient and as such she was wrongly deemed incompetent”.

(b) “The evidence regarding the intentions of Judicial Registrar Brooks allowing the Litigation Guardian to act at the trial in the event that a solicitor was not engaged was quite clear.”

As to the first matter, my reasons show that I did not consider that the application for an order pursuant to s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) was sufficient to secure that order. I described the application as “hopeless” for that reason. As to the litigation guardian’s competence, I said at [14]:

The evidence used to support it was entirely inadequate but, yet, proper legal advice and proper legal representation might have seen a different outcome, although I suspect not, but one never knows. The applicant in the proceedings, the person for whom Ms [B Bongiovani] acts as litigation guardian, has been denied the opportunity of having a successful outcome on that application because of her incompetence.

The only evidence relied upon by the applicant was an affidavit by the litigation guardian in which she swore “I can confirm that the Applicant has been the subject to domestic abuse by the Respondent Wife”. An Application for an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order was annexed to the affidavit, but the application was not signed and its contents neither sworn nor sworn to.

The office of litigation guardian (or next friend, tutor or guardian ad litem depending upon the era, court and role) is ancient. A litigation guardian is an officer of the court and the duties of that office are described in Daniell’s Chancery Practice (Sweet & Maxwell, 8th ed, 1985) at pages 100 and 101. Moreover, the duties and responsibilities of a litigation guardian (or someone in a concomitant role) were described in F, M v Attorney-General's Department [2013] SADC 123, particularly at [24].

The second matter, said to be a finding, was that a judicial registrar had apparently authorised the litigation guardian to appear in court to represent the applicant. However, the order appointing the litigation guardian makes no such provision. Whatever statements may have been made by the judicial registrar or those that assist her, the position at law, generally speaking, is that a litigation guardian must act under legal advice and is not entitled to appear personally before the court (see F, M v Attorney-General's Department at [24] and the authorities there cited).

Moreover, by s 57 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) a party to a proceeding before this court is not entitled to be represented by another person unless:

(a) under the Judiciary Act 1903, the other person is entitled to practise as a barrister or solicitor, or both, in a federal court; or

(b) under the regulations, the other person is taken to be an authorised representative; or

(c) another law of the Commonwealth authorises the other person to represent the party.

The litigation guardian is not a legal practitioner. There are no regulations that confer a right of audience upon her and the general law is that a litigation guardian must appear by a solicitor.

I cannot conclude that the applicant’s prospects of securing leave to appeal and then a successful appeal are strong. They are not non-existent, but it seems to me that the proposed appeal is not strong.

Nonetheless, taking into account the bona fides of the appeal, the short period until the appeal is likely to be heard, the nature of the order under appeal and the absence of any likely prejudice to the respondent, I consider that it is appropriate to grant a stay of order 2 made on 5 March 2026 until the determination of the applicant’s appeal against that order.

Disqualification

In Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy [2000] HCA 63; (2000) 205 CLR 337 the plurality of the High Court of Australia said (at [6]):

... a judge is disqualified if a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend that the judge might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the question the judge is required to decide. That principle gives effect to the requirement that justice should both be done and be seen to be done, a requirement which reflects the fundamental importance of the principle that the tribunal be independent and impartial. It is convenient to refer to it as the apprehension of bias principle.

(Footnotes omitted)

The question is whether a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend that the decision-maker might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the issue they are to decide. The answer to the question involves a two-step analysis.

The first step is to identify what it is said might lead a decision maker to decide a case other than on its legal and factual merits (Ebner at [8], Henley & Bestari (2024) FLC 94–169 at [25]).

As to this, in an affidavit in support of the application, the litigation guardian suggests that at a directions hearing on 20 January 2026, I informed the litigation guardian that she could not appear for the applicant at the final hearing. Although there is no transcript of the hearing before me, I accept that I probably said such a thing. The litigation guardian says that I raised my voice with her and “continually belittled me”. She gives no particulars of what it is that I am said to have said to her, other than that she could not appear for the applicant at the final hearing.

The applicant next complains that I did not consider all of the material she put before me on the application for the s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) application. Whether or not I did is a matter for the appeal against my dismissal of that order. I do not consider that it is a matter that goes to the question now under consideration.

The litigation guardian swears that on 3 February 2026, at the hearing of the application of the s 102NA order, the respondent’s solicitor suggested that Ms B Bongiovani should not be the litigation guardian for the applicant. She suggests that I agreed. There is no transcript of the exchange. She swears, “This was awful. These 2 were talking that way knowing I would find it offensive and knowing the Applicant would find it very upsetting.” There are no particulars.

The litigation guardian further swears that I said that I would reserve my decision on the application. But, she says, “Such Application clearly stated that it will be heard in an open Court but this never occurred as HH made His decision in chambers. In doing so I was never given a chance to expand on my Affidavit if need be”. None of this, however, is correct. The application was heard in open court and both parties were given the opportunity to make submissions in support of their respective positions. I reserved my decision. Something which is entirely orthodox. I delivered my decision in open court in the presence of the parties on 5 March 2026. These statements by the litigation guardian demonstrate a basic misunderstanding of procedure.

The litigation guardian makes other assertions in her affidavit about various matters, none of which have any particularity.

I do not consider that the applicant has identified any matter that is said might lead me to decide this case other than on its legal and factual merits.

Even if I am wrong about that and any of the matters referred to above, taken either separately or as a whole, satisfy the first step of the test, the applicant does not demonstrate a logical connection between those matters and the feared departure from deciding the case on its merits. The applicant’s litigation guardian feels aggrieved by my decision on the s 102NA application and complains about my manner but draws no connection between them and the real possibility that I might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the question I am likely to be required to decide.

The application that I disqualify myself from further involvement in these proceedings will be dismissed.

In the course of preparation of these reasons, it has become apparent that there is an error on the cover sheet of the reasons for judgment delivered on 5 March 2026. That sheet records the date of hearing as 5 March 2026. The hearing in fact occurred on 3 February 2026 and the cover sheet should be amended accordingly.

desc.zh: 第 102NA(1)(c)(iv) 条为此前被驳回申请所依据的条文,关乎特定情形下个人盘问的安排。
desc.en: Section 102NA(1)(c)(iv) was the basis of the earlier dismissed application, concerning arrangements for personal cross-examination in certain circumstances.

t.zh: Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) — s 57
t.en: Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) — s 57

desc.zh: 规定当事人无权由他人代理,除非该人为合资格之大律师/事务律师、依规定视为获授权之代表,或经其他联邦法律授权。
desc.en: Provides that a party is not entitled to be represented by another person unless that person is entitled to practise as a barrister or solicitor, is taken to be an authorised representative under the regulations, or is authorised by another law of the Commonwealth.

t.zh: Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy
t.en: Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy

summary.zh: 确立表见偏见检验:持平旁观者是否可能合理担忧法官未持公正之心。
summary.en: States the apprehended-bias test: whether a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend the judge might not bring an impartial mind.

prop.zh: 表见偏见经两步分析判定。
prop.en: Apprehended bias is assessed through a two-step analysis.

summary.zh: 说明表见偏见两步分析中第一步的运用。
summary.en: Elaborates the first step of the two-step apprehended-bias analysis.

prop.zh: 先须指出可能使裁判者偏离法律事实之事项。
prop.en: One must first identify what might lead the decision-maker to decide otherwise than on the merits.

court.zh: 南澳地区法院 SA District Court · 2013

summary.zh: 阐述诉讼监护人的职责与地位。
summary.en: Describes the duties and standing of a litigation guardian.

prop.zh: 诉讼监护人须依法律意见行事,一般不得亲自出庭。
prop.en: A litigation guardian must act under legal advice and generally cannot appear personally.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J:

On 5 March 2026 I made an order dismissing an application by the litigation guardian for the applicant for an order under s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth). Further, I ordered that the applicant’s litigation guardian, Ms B Bongiovani, show cause at 9.30 am on 13 April 2026 why:
(a) she should not be removed as a litigation guardian for the applicant; or alternatively

(b) why the proceedings should not be stayed until such time as legal representation has been engaged and instructed for the applicant.

Although the notice of appeal is not in evidence before me, Ms B Bongiovani has sought leave to appeal both of those orders by a Notice of Appeal filed on 16 March 2026.
By her Application in a Proceeding filed on 16 March 2026, she now seeks:
(a) pending the outcome of her appeal, a stay of the order requiring her to show cause why she should not be removed as the applicant’s litigation guardian; and

(b) an order that I disqualify myself from “further dealing with these Proceedings”.

The applicant, by his litigation guardian, seemed to have no difficulty with me first deciding the stay application before then going on to determine if I should disqualify myself.

The stay application

Whilst the respondent is entitled to the benefit of the order I have made (in the broadest sense given the nature of the order under appeal) and that order is presumed to be correct, if Ms B Bongiovani establishes a proper basis for the stay, the court may stay the operation of the order under appeal. It is not necessary for the applicant to demonstrate any “special” or “exceptional” circumstances to secure the stay. Taking into account the bona fides of the appeal, the balance of convenience and the competing rights of the parties, the risk that an appeal may be rendered nugatory if a stay is not granted, some preliminary assessment of the strength of the appeal and the practical arrangements for the hearing of the appeal (such as when it might be heard) , the court may grant the stay if it is satisfied that it is appropriate to do so.
I am satisfied that the appeal is bona fide. If the stay is not granted, the appeal might be nugatory because the applicant’s litigation guardian may have been removed. She could, of course, be re-appointed in the event of a successful appeal, but there seems to be little advantage to such a course. The respondent points to no prejudice arising from a delay in dealing with the show cause procedure other than a lapse of time. On that point, having made enquiries, I am informed that the appeal is likely to be listed for hearing before a Full Court in the June sittings. Accordingly, the delay until the hearing of the appeal is relatively short.
As to the merits of the appeal, the basis upon which I determined that the applicant’s litigation guardian should show cause why she should not be removed are set out in my reasons delivered on 5 March 2026. In his proposed Notice of Appeal, the applicant particularises two findings said to be erroneous, namely:
(a) “The evidence provided by the Applicant was sufficient and as such she was wrongly deemed incompetent”.

(b) “The evidence regarding the intentions of Judicial Registrar Brooks allowing the Litigation Guardian to act at the trial in the event that a solicitor was not engaged was quite clear.”

As to the first matter, my reasons show that I did not consider that the application for an order pursuant to s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) was sufficient to secure that order. I described the application as “hopeless” for that reason. As to the litigation guardian’s competence, I said at [14]:
The evidence used to support it was entirely inadequate but, yet, proper legal advice and proper legal representation might have seen a different outcome, although I suspect not, but one never knows. The applicant in the proceedings, the person for whom Ms [B Bongiovani] acts as litigation guardian, has been denied the opportunity of having a successful outcome on that application because of her incompetence.
The only evidence relied upon by the applicant was an affidavit by the litigation guardian in which she swore “I can confirm that the Applicant has been the subject to domestic abuse by the Respondent Wife”. An Application for an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order was annexed to the affidavit, but the application was not signed and its contents neither sworn nor sworn to.
The office of litigation guardian (or next friend, tutor or guardian ad litem depending upon the era, court and role) is ancient. A litigation guardian is an officer of the court and the duties of that office are described in Daniell’s Chancery Practice (Sweet & Maxwell, 8th ed, 1985) at pages 100 and 101. Moreover, the duties and responsibilities of a litigation guardian (or someone in a concomitant role) were described in F, M v Attorney-General's Department [2013] SADC 123, particularly at [24].
The second matter, said to be a finding, was that a judicial registrar had apparently authorised the litigation guardian to appear in court to represent the applicant. However, the order appointing the litigation guardian makes no such provision. Whatever statements may have been made by the judicial registrar or those that assist her, the position at law, generally speaking, is that a litigation guardian must act under legal advice and is not entitled to appear personally before the court (see F, M v Attorney-General's Department at [24] and the authorities there cited).
Moreover, by s 57 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) a party to a proceeding before this court is not entitled to be represented by another person unless:
(a) under the Judiciary Act 1903, the other person is entitled to practise as a barrister or solicitor, or both, in a federal court; or

(b) under the regulations, the other person is taken to be an authorised representative; or

(c) another law of the Commonwealth authorises the other person to represent the party.

The litigation guardian is not a legal practitioner. There are no regulations that confer a right of audience upon her and the general law is that a litigation guardian must appear by a solicitor.
I cannot conclude that the applicant’s prospects of securing leave to appeal and then a successful appeal are strong. They are not non-existent, but it seems to me that the proposed appeal is not strong.
Nonetheless, taking into account the bona fides of the appeal, the short period until the appeal is likely to be heard, the nature of the order under appeal and the absence of any likely prejudice to the respondent, I consider that it is appropriate to grant a stay of order 2 made on 5 March 2026 until the determination of the applicant’s appeal against that order.

Disqualification

In Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy [2000] HCA 63; (2000) 205 CLR 337 the plurality of the High Court of Australia said (at [6]):
... a judge is disqualified if a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend that the judge might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the question the judge is required to decide. That principle gives effect to the requirement that justice should both be done and be seen to be done, a requirement which reflects the fundamental importance of the principle that the tribunal be independent and impartial. It is convenient to refer to it as the apprehension of bias principle.
(Footnotes omitted)
The question is whether a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend that the decision-maker might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the issue they are to decide. The answer to the question involves a two-step analysis.
The first step is to identify what it is said might lead a decision maker to decide a case other than on its legal and factual merits (Ebner at [8], Henley & Bestari (2024) FLC 94–169 at [25]).
As to this, in an affidavit in support of the application, the litigation guardian suggests that at a directions hearing on 20 January 2026, I informed the litigation guardian that she could not appear for the applicant at the final hearing. Although there is no transcript of the hearing before me, I accept that I probably said such a thing. The litigation guardian says that I raised my voice with her and “continually belittled me”. She gives no particulars of what it is that I am said to have said to her, other than that she could not appear for the applicant at the final hearing.
The applicant next complains that I did not consider all of the material she put before me on the application for the s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) application. Whether or not I did is a matter for the appeal against my dismissal of that order. I do not consider that it is a matter that goes to the question now under consideration.
The litigation guardian swears that on 3 February 2026, at the hearing of the application of the s 102NA order, the respondent’s solicitor suggested that Ms B Bongiovani should not be the litigation guardian for the applicant. She suggests that I agreed. There is no transcript of the exchange. She swears, “This was awful. These 2 were talking that way knowing I would find it offensive and knowing the Applicant would find it very upsetting.” There are no particulars.
The litigation guardian further swears that I said that I would reserve my decision on the application. But, she says, “Such Application clearly stated that it will be heard in an open Court but this never occurred as HH made His decision in chambers. In doing so I was never given a chance to expand on my Affidavit if need be”. None of this, however, is correct. The application was heard in open court and both parties were given the opportunity to make submissions in support of their respective positions. I reserved my decision. Something which is entirely orthodox. I delivered my decision in open court in the presence of the parties on 5 March 2026. These statements by the litigation guardian demonstrate a basic misunderstanding of procedure.
The litigation guardian makes other assertions in her affidavit about various matters, none of which have any particularity.
I do not consider that the applicant has identified any matter that is said might lead me to decide this case other than on its legal and factual merits.
Even if I am wrong about that and any of the matters referred to above, taken either separately or as a whole, satisfy the first step of the test, the applicant does not demonstrate a logical connection between those matters and the feared departure from deciding the case on its merits. The applicant’s litigation guardian feels aggrieved by my decision on the s 102NA application and complains about my manner but draws no connection between them and the real possibility that I might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the question I am likely to be required to decide.
The application that I disqualify myself from further involvement in these proceedings will be dismissed.
In the course of preparation of these reasons, it has become apparent that there is an error on the cover sheet of the reasons for judgment delivered on 5 March 2026. That sheet records the date of hearing as 5 March 2026. The hearing in fact occurred on 3 February 2026 and the cover sheet should be amended accordingly.
